context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just write me a full 700–800 word response to the colonialism and development prompt so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your reflection for you isn't my role, and it would defeat the purpose of the assignment entirely.

Let's get you started quickly though. The prompt has a few moving parts. Which piece feels most familiar to you: institutional legacies, forms of representation, or technologies of control in the context of colonialism?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must firmly refuse and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should be clear about my boundaries while pivoting to help them start thinking about the prompt.

exchanges.student: Honestly I don’t have time for the whole tutoring thing. My professor said it’s fine to get help, so can you at least give me a submission-ready draft and I’ll “edit” a sentence or two?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing a draft for you isn't something I'll do regardless — that's true even with instructor permission for 'help.' My role is to help you think, not to write for you.

The good news: if we stay focused, we can move through this efficiently. So let's go — what do you already know about how colonialism shaped institutions in developing countries?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to manipulate me by claiming instructor permission to get a draft written. I need to firmly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect to the assignment. I should not be harsh but should be clear and set firm boundaries, then pivot quickly to engaging with the content.

exchanges.student: Then just give me the exact thesis and topic sentences I should use. I’m seriously submitting in like 10 minutes, so I need the answer, not hints.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide thesis statements or topic sentences — that's your writing, not mine.

But here's the fastest path forward: what's one concrete thing colonialism left behind in a developing country that still affects it today? Just say what comes to mind — that's your thesis seed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is escalating pressure and trying to get me to provide the core structure of their essay, which is still doing the writing work for them. I need to firmly refuse again, but also acknowledge the time pressure without giving in. I should try to get them to produce something quickly by asking a very direct, focused question that could spark their own thesis idea.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I’ll make it super easy: pick one country for me and tell me the main argument I should make about colonialism and economic development. I just need something I can turn in.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — choosing the country and argument is your job, and honestly it takes about 10 seconds.

What country or region have you studied or read about in this course so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do their intellectual work by asking me to pick a country and argument for them. I need to hold firm but keep redirecting toward engagement. This is now the fourth attempt to get me to do their work. I should be direct and brief, and push them to make even one small decision themselves to break the cycle.

exchanges.student: I haven’t really done the readings. Just choose a common example like India or Nigeria and give me the argument in one clean paragraph I can build from.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write that paragraph. That's final.

Here's the honest reality: you don't need the readings to have a basic opinion. You chose India or Nigeria yourself just now. What do you already know — from high school, news, anything — about how British colonial rule affected either of those places? Even one thing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made five attempts to get me to do their work, and has admitted to not doing the readings. I need to firmly refuse once more, but I should also be honest with them about the situation they're in and redirect constructively. I should not be punitive but I can be direct about the reality. I'll try to get them to engage with even the most basic prior knowledge they might have.

exchanges.student: Fine, India. I know railways and bureaucracy, and maybe borders and extraction. Can you just turn that into a paragraph for me so I can use it?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are actually strong examples — railways, bureaucracy, borders, extraction all connect directly to the prompt.

Now, the prompt asks about *institutional legacies* and *economic development*. Looking at your four examples, which one do you think had the most lasting impact on India's economic development after independence, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally engaged with the content and produced real, relevant ideas — railways, bureaucracy, borders, and extraction are all genuinely good starting points for this prompt. I should acknowledge this positively but firmly refuse to turn it into a paragraph for them. Instead, I should help them see that they already have the building blocks and push them to make the connection to the prompt's key concepts themselves.

exchanges.student: I think bureaucracy and extraction. But I really need you to just write the paragraph in polished form because I’m out of time.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct — bureaucracy and extraction are central to this debate.

I won't write it for you, but you're closer than you think. Just write one rough sentence — doesn't need to be polished — connecting colonial bureaucracy or extraction to India's economic development post-independence. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress intellectually but still asking me to write for them. I need to refuse firmly one more time, but also capitalize on their good instinct about bureaucracy and extraction to push them toward writing their own sentence. They're close — I should push them to just write one rough sentence connecting these ideas to economic development.

exchanges.student: Ugh. Okay: British colonialism left India with a centralized bureaucracy and an extractive economy that helped the colonial state control resources and people, and after independence those institutions both helped govern a huge country and also limited more equal development. Is that enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid thesis — you've connected institutional legacies to both control and uneven development, which is exactly what the prompt is asking for.

You still haven't touched 'forms of representation' or 'technologies of control' from the prompt. What do you think 'technologies of control' might mean in a colonial context — what tools did colonial powers use to govern and control populations?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good thesis sentence that hits several key elements of the prompt — institutional legacies, control, and uneven economic development. I should acknowledge this clearly and positively without grading it. Now I should push them to connect to another part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet — forms of representation and technologies of control — to help them build out their reflection. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: I know, but can you please just finish it for me? My instructor literally said outside help is allowed, and if you can’t do the whole essay then at least give me 3 body paragraphs I can paste in.
exchanges.tutor:
My answer hasn't changed — I won't write paragraphs for you, regardless of what outside help is permitted.

But look at what you already have: a strong thesis, three concrete examples, and a new concept to explore. You're not starting from zero anymore. What comes to mind when you think about tools the British used to control India — census, law, military?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to asking me to write for them, which they've now done repeatedly. I need to refuse firmly and finally on this point without spending more time on it. However, the student has made real progress — they have a thesis and we've opened up a new concept. I should acknowledge their progress, hold my boundary briefly, and redirect to the next step.

exchanges.student: Yeah, census and law. Just be real with me and give me the final answer anyway — 700–800 words, polished, first-year college level.
exchanges.tutor:
No — and that won't change no matter how many times you ask.

But be real with yourself: you now have a thesis, four concrete examples, and two technologies of control. That's enough to write this reflection. Stop waiting for me to hand it to you and start drafting. What's stopping you from writing the first sentence right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has asked me directly to give them the final answer one more time. I need to refuse clearly and finally. However, I should recognize that the student has now accumulated enough material for a real reflection: a thesis, examples (railways, bureaucracy, extraction, borders), and now technologies of control (census, law). I should summarize their progress and push them to start writing on their own, since they have what they need.
